The **Supreme Court of New South Wales** is the highest state [court](/wiki/Court "Court") of the Australian state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"). It has unlimited [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") within the state in [civil](/wiki/Civil_law_(common_law) "Civil law (common law)") matters, and hears the most serious [criminal](/wiki/Criminal_law "Criminal law") matters. Whilst the Supreme Court is the highest New South Wales court in the [Australian court hierarchy](/wiki/Australian_court_hierarchy "Australian court hierarchy"), an appeal by special leave can be made to the [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia").

Matters of [appeal](/wiki/Appeal "Appeal") can be submitted to the [New South Wales Court of Appeal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Court_of_Appeal "New South Wales Court of Appeal") and [Court of Criminal Appeal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Court_of_Criminal_Appeal "New South Wales Court of Criminal Appeal"), both of which are constituted by members of the Supreme Court, in the case of the Court of Appeal from those who have been commissioned as judges of appeal.

The Supreme Court consists of 52 permanent judges, including the [Chief Justice of New South Wales](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_New_South_Wales "Chief Justice of New South Wales"), currently [Andrew Bell](/wiki/Andrew_Bell_(judge) "Andrew Bell (judge)"), the President of the Court of Appeal, 10 Judges of Appeal, the Chief Judge at Common Law, and the Chief Judge in Equity.

The Supreme Court's central location is the Law Courts Building in [Queen's Square, Sydney](/wiki/Queen%27s_Square,_Sydney "Queen's Square, Sydney").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

The first [superior court](/wiki/Superior_court "Superior court") of the [Colony of New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales#1788—British_settlement "New South Wales") (known as the [Supreme Court of Civil Judicature](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Civil_Judicature_of_New_South_Wales "Supreme Court of Civil Judicature of New South Wales")) was established by [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") dated 2 April 1814, known as the *Second Charter of Justice of New South Wales*. That [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter") provided that there should be a Supreme Court constituted by a Judge appointed by the King's commission and two Magistrates. The charter also created the Governor's Court and the Lieutenant-Governor's Court. The jurisdiction of the Governor's Court and the Supreme Court extended to [Van Diemen's Land](/wiki/Van_Diemen%27s_Land "Van Diemen's Land") (the former name for [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania")). All three courts were concerned with civil matters only.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Establishment")]

Legislation to establish a new supreme court for both New South Wales and Van Diemen's Land was prepared in [London](/wiki/London "London") by [James Stephen](/wiki/James_Stephen_(undersecretary) "James Stephen (undersecretary)"), counsel to the [Colonial Office](/wiki/Colonial_Office "Colonial Office"), and [Francis Forbes](/wiki/Francis_Forbes "Francis Forbes"), Chief Justice of [Newfoundland](/wiki/Colony_of_Newfoundland "Colony of Newfoundland") and Chief Justice-designate of New South Wales. The act was called an "Act to provide for the better administration of justice in New South Wales and Van Diemen's Land and for the more effectual government thereof" and is numbered as [4 Geo. 4](/wiki/4_Geo._4 "4 Geo. 4"). c. 96. The statute was passed on 19 July 1823.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In consequence of this legislation, [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") establishing the New South Wales Supreme Court were sealed on 13 October 1823, and proclaimed in Sydney on 17 May 1824. They are known as the Third Charter of Justice of New South Wales.

This charter provided that there should be a [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice "Chief Justice") for the colony of New South Wales in the Island of [New Holland](/wiki/New_Holland_(Australia) "New Holland (Australia)") (as the continent of Australia was then known), as well as other judges, a [registrar](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/registrar "wikt:registrar"), a [prothonotary](/wiki/Prothonotary "Prothonotary"), a [master](/wiki/Master_(judiciary) "Master (judiciary)"), and a Keeper of Records and such other Officers as may be necessary for the administration of Justice in the colony.

The charter also established the office of [sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff "Sheriff"); gave precedence to the Chief Justice over all other subjects except the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales") (or acting Governor) of the colony; and allowed the Court to admit persons to be [barristers](/wiki/Barrister "Barrister"), [attorneys](/wiki/Lawyer "Lawyer"), [proctors](/wiki/Proctor "Proctor") or [solicitors](/wiki/Solicitor "Solicitor") as the case may be. Previously, a person had to be admitted as such in the United Kingdom. However, ex-convicts were not permitted to be admitted.

In 1840, a [Port Phillip division](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales_for_the_District_of_Port_Phillip "Supreme Court of New South Wales for the District of Port Phillip") of the Court was created, consisting of a single Resident Judge, to exercise the court's jurisdiction in the [Port Phillip District](/wiki/Port_Phillip_District "Port Phillip District") of the Colony of New South Wales.[[4]](#cite_note-bennett_scnsw-4) The division existed until 1852, when it was replaced by the [Supreme Court of Victoria](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Victoria "Supreme Court of Victoria") following the creation of the [Colony of Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)").[[5]](#cite_note-bennett_à_beckett-5)

Also in 1840, the [Parliament of New South Wales](/wiki/Parliament_of_New_South_Wales "Parliament of New South Wales") established a separate [equity](/wiki/Equity_(law) "Equity (law)") division in the court. Limited [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") in [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce") cases was granted in 1873 and full [Admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty_law "Admiralty law") jurisdiction was added in 1911. The Supreme Court, in 1972, was one of the last [Common Law](/wiki/Common_Law "Common Law") jurisdictions in the world to fuse the administration of Equity and Common Law, although these continue as the historic names for the two divisions of the court. This process began in the United Kingdom with the passage of the [Judicature Acts](/wiki/Judicature_Acts "Judicature Acts") in 1873. Since 1930, three generations of the [Street family](/wiki/Street_family "Street family") have served New South Wales as Chief Justice.

Supreme Court Judges [Carolyn Simpson](/wiki/Carolyn_Simpson_(judge) "Carolyn Simpson (judge)"), [Margaret Beazley](/wiki/Margaret_Beazley "Margaret Beazley") and [Virginia Bell](/wiki/Virginia_Bell_(judge) "Virginia Bell (judge)") made headlines in April 1999 when the three sat in the [Court of Criminal Appeal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Court_of_Criminal_Appeal "New South Wales Court of Criminal Appeal") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney").[[6]](#cite_note-Alumninews-6) The Judges threw out an [appeal](/wiki/Appeal "Appeal") from a convicted [computer hacker](/wiki/Hacker_(computer_security) "Hacker (computer security)") who had, out of "sheer maliciousness", been posting offensive messages on Ausnet's homepage. According to the Women Lawyers Association of NSW, there had never been an all-female bench in [England](/wiki/England "England") or [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") at the time.[[7]](#cite_note-Gazette-7)

## Structure and jurisdiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Structure and jurisdiction")]

The court now operates under the *[Constitution Act 1902](https://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/nsw/consol_act/ca1902188/)* (NSW), the *[Supreme Court Act 1970](https://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/nsw/consol_act/sca1970183/)* (NSW), and the *[Civil Procedure Act 2005](https://www.austlii.edu.au/au/legis/nsw/consol_act/cpa2005167/)* (NSW), although provisions on the appointment and removal of judicial officers were incorporated into the state's [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") in 1992.

The court consists of 52 permanent judges, three Acting Judges of Appeal, two Acting Judges, and an Associate Judge. Permanent judges include the [Chief Justice of New South Wales](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_New_South_Wales "Chief Justice of New South Wales"), the President of the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Court_of_Appeal "New South Wales Court of Appeal"), eleven Judges of Appeal (one of whom is currently the Chief Judge at Common Law), the Chief Judge at Common Law and the Chief Judge in Equity, and 38 [Puisne](/wiki/Puisne "Puisne") Judges.

The Chief Judge in each trial division also sits in the Court of Appeal from time to time. Occasionally, puisne judges also sit in the Court of Appeal, though this is uncommon.

The court hears very serious cases such as murder and treason, civil cases involving more than $750 000, and civil matters such as wills, injunctions, and admiralty. The court's work at first instance is divided between the Common Law Division, which hears civil, criminal and administrative law matters, and the Equity Division, which hears equity, probate, commercial, admiralty, and protective matters. The court includes the Court of Appeal and the Court of Criminal Appeal which hear appeals from the [District Court](/wiki/District_Court_of_New_South_Wales "District Court of New South Wales") and the [Local Court](/wiki/Local_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Local Court of New South Wales") and from single judges sitting in the Common Law or Equity Divisions. The Court of Appeal also hears appeals from the [Land and Environment Court of New South Wales](/wiki/Land_and_Environment_Court_of_New_South_Wales "Land and Environment Court of New South Wales") and a number of administrative tribunals.

The Court of Appeal and the Court of Criminal Appeal are respectively the highest civil and criminal courts in the state. To appeal to the [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") from the Court of Appeal or the Court of Criminal Appeal, special leave must be granted by the High Court.

Appeals from state supreme courts to the High Court are not limited to matters in which a [federal question](/wiki/Federal_question "Federal question") arises and the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Australia "Constitution of Australia") empowers the [Federal Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia") to make laws vesting state courts with federal jurisdiction. The High Court of Australia can review decisions of the Supreme Court of New South Wales in relation to the common law and equitable jurisdictions of the court as well. The High Court of Australia has exercised this power on a number of occasions.

## Court buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Court buildings")]

The Supreme Court of New South Wales was proclaimed in Sydney on 17 May 1824.

### Greenway Wing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Greenway Wing")]

Main article: [Greenway Wing (Supreme Court of New South Wales)](/wiki/Greenway_Wing_(Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales) "Greenway Wing (Supreme Court of New South Wales)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/King_Street_Court_House%2C_Sydney.jpg/250px-King_Street_Court_House%2C_Sydney.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Street_Court_House,_Sydney.jpg)

[Greenway Wing](/wiki/Greenway_Wing_(Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales) "Greenway Wing (Supreme Court of New South Wales)") on King Street

The inaugural Supreme Court building that is located on the corner of [King Street](/wiki/King_Street,_Sydney "King Street, Sydney") and [Elizabeth Street](/wiki/Elizabeth_Street,_Sydney "Elizabeth Street, Sydney") in the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district"), adjacent to what is now known as [Queen's Square](/wiki/Queen%27s_Square,_Sydney "Queen's Square, Sydney"), was built between 1820 and 1828. The two-storey rectangular [Georgian](/wiki/Australian_non-residential_architectural_styles#Georgian "Australian non-residential architectural styles") building, with an additional [loggia](/wiki/Loggia "Loggia") and [cornice](/wiki/Cornice "Cornice") added in 1868, was designed by [Francis Greenway](/wiki/Francis_Greenway "Francis Greenway") in 1819 under the direction of [Governor Macquarie](/wiki/Lachlan_Macquarie "Lachlan Macquarie"). This building is now called the Greenway Wing. Greenway was dismissed before the building was completed and its design was so modified by his successor, [Standish Lawrence Harris](/wiki/Standish_Lawrence_Harris "Standish Lawrence Harris"), that the building barely resembles his original design. The building was occupied by the Supreme Court from 1827. In the 1860s [James Barnet](/wiki/James_Barnet "James Barnet") designed additions for the building including an arcaded loggia along the King Street [façade](/wiki/Fa%C3%A7ade "Façade") and the new classical cornice and [parapet](/wiki/Parapet "Parapet") for the roof, giving the structure a [Victorian Italianate](/wiki/Australian_non-residential_architectural_styles#Victorian_Italianate "Australian non-residential architectural styles") appearance. The building was added to the [New South Wales State Heritage Register](/wiki/New_South_Wales_State_Heritage_Register "New South Wales State Heritage Register") on 2 April 1999.[[8]](#cite_note-nswshr-800-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Greenway was also responsible for designing the nearby [Hyde Park Barracks](/wiki/Hyde_Park_Barracks,_Sydney "Hyde Park Barracks, Sydney"), recorded on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List"), and [St James' Church](/wiki/St_James%27_Church,_Sydney "St James' Church, Sydney"), listed on the New South Wales State Heritage Register.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Old Registry Wing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Old Registry Wing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/1_Old_Registry_Office.JPG/250px-1_Old_Registry_Office.JPG)](/wiki/File:1_Old_Registry_Office.JPG)

The Old Registry building, the second structure to house the Court, completed in 1862, looking east across Elizabeth Street.

Main article: [Old Registry Wing (Supreme Court of New South Wales)](/wiki/Old_Registry_Wing_(Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales) "Old Registry Wing (Supreme Court of New South Wales)")

The second structure to house the Court is located immediately the southwest of the Greenway Wing on the corner of Elizabeth Street and St James Road. Designed by [Government Architect](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Government_Architect "New South Wales Government Architect") [Alexander Dawson](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Government_Architect#Alexander_Dawson_(1856–1862) "New South Wales Government Architect"), it is one of only two Government buildings which were designed in the [Victorian Free Gothic](/wiki/Australian_non-residential_architectural_styles#Victorian_Free_Gothic "Australian non-residential architectural styles") style, the other being the nearby [Land Titles Office](/wiki/Registrar-General%27s_building "Registrar-General's building"). Built between 1859 and 1862 and listed on the New South Wales State Heritage Register on 2 April 1999, the site is now known as the Old Registry Building. Barnet extended the Old Registry building in 1875 and 1886.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Banco Court wing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Banco Court wing")]

Main article: [Banco Court (Supreme Court of New South Wales)](/wiki/Banco_Court_(Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales) "Banco Court (Supreme Court of New South Wales)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/St_James_Road_Court_%28Banco_Court%29_NSW_Supreme_Court_20190104.jpg/250px-St_James_Road_Court_%28Banco_Court%29_NSW_Supreme_Court_20190104.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_James_Road_Court_(Banco_Court)_NSW_Supreme_Court_20190104.jpg)

Banco Road Court

Designed by [Walter Liberty Vernon](/wiki/Walter_Liberty_Vernon "Walter Liberty Vernon") and built between 1895 and 1896 in the [Federation Free Classical](/wiki/Australian_non-residential_architectural_styles#Federation_Free_Classical "Australian non-residential architectural styles") style, the two-storey rich red brick Banco Court building was the third location of the Supreme Court. The Banco Wing is located to the east of the Old Registry building on St James Road and south of the Greenway Wing and makes little reference to the earlier buildings in either style or detailing. The interior of the courtroom has aesthetic significance and is said to be modelled on St Stephen's Court in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"). The court building is located in St James' Road, opposite the north-western edge of [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney"). Although in some sources it is referred to as "Banco Road Court", the origin of this alternative name is unknown - there is no Banco Road.

### Law Courts Building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Supreme_Court_of_New_South_Wales&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Law Courts Building")]

Main article: [Law Courts Building, Sydney](/wiki/Law_Courts_Building,_Sydney "Law Courts Building, Sydney")

In 1976 the New South Wales Government completed construction of the Sydney Law Courts building, facing Queen's Square and bounded by [Phillip](/wiki/Phillip_Street "Phillip Street") and [Macquarie](/wiki/Macquarie_Street,_Sydney "Macquarie Street, Sydney") streets. The 27-storey 33,000-square-metre (360,000 sq ft) building is owned by Law Courts Limited, a company whose shareholders comprise the [Government of Australia](/wiki/Government_of_Australia "Government of Australia") and the [Government of New South Wales](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales"). The building houses the [High Court of Australia](/wiki/High_Court_of_Australia "High Court of Australia") (when it sits in Sydney), the [Federal Court of Australia](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Australia "Federal Court of Australia") and the NSW Supreme Court. The building was designed by architects McConnel Smith and Johnson and received an [RAIA](/wiki/Australian_Institute_of_Architects "Australian Institute of Architects") Merit Award in 1977 and stands as a strong, singular statement representative of its time and a product of the [brutalist](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture") school of architecture. Refurbished in 2009 at a cost of A$214 million, a range of sustainability measures were implemented to extend the life and amenity of the building.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
